title: Reconcile CRM and Billing Accounts
subtitle: Use FULL OUTER JOIN to classify matched and unmatched records
learning_objective: Retain every account from two systems and identify where each account exists.
recommended_lesson: Lesson 4: FULL OUTER and CROSS JOIN
why: Data migrations and audits require both left-only and right-only records, not just matches.
prompt: Return `account_id`, `crm_name`, `billing_name`, and `source_status`. Use `Matched`, `CRM only`, or `Billing only` to classify each account. Order by `account_id`.
expected_result: 5 rows for account IDs 1, 2, 3, 4, and 7. IDs 1, 2, and 7 are Matched; 3 is CRM only; 4 is Billing only.
build_steps: FULL OUTER JOIN the two account tables on `account_id`.
Use COALESCE to produce one account ID.
Use CASE to inspect which side has a non-null ID.
Order by the combined account ID alias.
explanation: Full outer join preserves both systems. NULLs indicate which source did not contain a matching account.
common_mistakes: Using INNER JOIN and hiding all discrepancies.
Selecting only the CRM account ID, which becomes NULL for billing-only rows.
Treating different account names as unmatched even though the IDs match.
hints: Check for both IDs first in CASE, then left-only, otherwise right-only.
takeaways: FULL OUTER JOIN is ideal for reconciliation.
COALESCE combines equivalent identifiers from either side.
reflection_questions: Which columns reveal that account 3 is CRM only?
stretch_goal: Add a `name_mismatch` flag for matched IDs whose two names differ.
starter_sql:
SELECT
    COALESCE(c.account_id, b.account_id) AS account_id,
    c.account_name AS crm_name,
    b.billing_name,
    CASE
        -- classify matched and unmatched rows
    END AS source_status
FROM crm_accounts AS c
FULL OUTER JOIN billing_accounts AS b
    ON c.account_id = b.account_id
ORDER BY account_id;
solution_explanation: Both tables are preserved; COALESCE selects the available ID and CASE classifies the NULL pattern.
solution_walkthrough: The full outer join creates matched, CRM-only, and billing-only rows.
COALESCE returns whichever account ID exists.
CASE checks the matched condition before one-sided conditions.
